EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm southerly flow allowed for temperatures to reach well into the 80s to near 90 on Wednesday, July 2nd. In addition, it was rather humid with dewpoints nearing 70 degrees. With the warm and humid conditions in place, the atmosphere became quite unstable during the afternoon and evening hours. ||Well ahead of a cold front, a surface trough was in place across upstate New York and western New England. This caused thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours. A few of these storms became severe, producing wind damage to trees and power lines.  In addition, the very humid air mass in place gave the storms the capability of producing very heavy rainfall in a short period of time. This allowed for one of the storms to produce flash flooding in the Pittsfield area, making travel difficult. ||These storms ended by the late evening hours, as nocturnal influences helped wane the development of additional thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed due to thunderstorm winds one mile south of South Egremont.  Route 41 was closed as a result of trees blocking the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place during the early morning hours on Wednesday, June 18th. A strong cold front moved across the region from west to east shortly after midnight during the early morning hours. Along and just ahead of this boundary, a line of severe thunderstorms in the form of a squall line raced from west to east across the area. These storms produced significant wind damage across eastern New York. The thunderstorms weakened somewhat as they reached the border with western New England as instability started to wane, but still caused damage to trees in a few locations. The threat for thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front by the late night hours, as drier and less humid air worked into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down in Lenox as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th. As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary. As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas. Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well. In addition, a few of the storms produced damaging lightning strikes as well. ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Great Barrington. A house on Main Street was impacted by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a slow moving cold front, a southerly flow transported plenty of moisture into the region during the day on Tuesday, June 3rd. With a moist and unstable air mass in place, plenty of slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the approaching boundary. Some of these thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in a short period of time. This led to some flash flooding, especially in urban and poor drainage areas. In addition, a few of the thunderstorms also produced strong wind gusts, which caused damage to trees and power lines. By the late evening, the threat for thunderstorms ended, as the frontal boundary pushed through and less humid air moved into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Lenox Branch Road in West Stockbridge was washed out due to flash flooding from thunderstorm heavy rainfall.

A cold front approaching southern New England brought scattered showers and thunderstorms to the region.  Most of these were ordinary thunderstorms, but one of them turned severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were downed along a 1.3 mile stretch of Richmond Road in Royalston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air over southern New England was enough to produce diurnal thunderstorms.  A few of these became severe producing both large hail and damaging winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Newell Hill Road was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and thunderstorms developed with the help of an upper level disturbance and afternoon heating.  Plenty of moisture was available resulting in heavy rainfall in a few areas.  This resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell in less than an hour in Framingham.  This rendered several streets flooded and impassable, including Route 9 westbound near Oakcrest Drive, portions of Concord, A, and School Street, Queens Way. Several cars became stuck in flood waters, in some cases trapping their occupants temporarily.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across New England in the evening. This combined with warm humid air and breaks of sunshine led to developing showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gilbert Street was flooded and impassable. Flood waters extended into a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across New England in the evening. This combined with warm humid air and breaks of sunshine led to developing showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touchdown occurred near the intersection of Perry Avenue and Fairfax Road. A narrow well-defined damage path extended past Saint Vincent Hospital to Gordon Street. The most significant damage was to trees, some of which were uprooted or snapped. Some trees landed on cars causing significant damage to the cars. A couple of trees landed on houses, but there was little structural damage to the houses. A few windows were broken by tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with an upper level disturbance to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England during the early morning hours of October 8th.  One of these storms produced a microburst in the town of Easthampton in western Massachusetts where numerous trees were downed on the west side of Mount Tom.  Scattered wind damage occurred elsewhere across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a strong microburst in Easthampton near the Holyoke line.  Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped off on the west side of Mount Tom.  A number of these trees were downed onto Route 141 and Mountain Road, trapping several cars on the roadway and causing Mountain Road to be closed by local officials.

Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England.  The strongest winds were along the east coast of Massachusetts where many trees were still fully leaved.  This resulted in significant tree damage along with power outages.  In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding, particularly across northeastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Nash Hill Road in Williamsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning and again in the afternoon as a slow moving frontal boundary crossed southern New England. A tropical airmass was in place which led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall produced significant urban flash flooding. In Waltham, the downtown area was flooded with many cars stranded, including Second Street, Cabot Street, Prospect Street, and Bacon Street. All eight lanes of Route 128 were flooded and impassable near Route 20, which led to gridlock for a few hours until the flood waters receded.  In Arlington, there were several road closures due to flooding in the area of Mystic Street and the Mystic Valley Parkway. In Lexington, Woburn Street and Massachusetts Avenue were flooded and closed. In Watertown, parts of Mount Auburn Street were flooded. In Medford, the area near Medford Square was flooded with water up to the engine blocks of cars.

In the midst of a long duration snow event, northerly winds transported very cold air  into the region from Canada on Thursday, January 2nd. Temperatures fell through the entire day, with many areas below zero by late in the day, even with snow falling. ||Temperatures continuned to hold steady or slowly fall into Friday morning on January 3rd. With winds picking up on Thursday night into Friday due a strengthening area of low pressure off the eastern seaboard, wind chill values became dangerously low. Wind chill values dropped below -15 degrees by Friday morning over much of the region. ||Wind chill values remained very low, even behind the storm through the day on Friday. With very cold temperatures continuing on Friday night, wind chill values continued to be -15 to -30 degrees in many locations. Winds finally became calm by early Saturday morning on January 4th, although the frigid temperatures continued to make for dangerous conditions.

The Bay Avenue sea wall opening lost its batter boards in the Avon Street area, which resulted in flooding. In Duxbury, Gurney Road was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptional winter storm impacted all of western Massachusetts between Thursday, February 13th and the morning of Friday, February 14th.||A strong area of low pressure across the Deep South moved towards the mid-Atlantic coast on February 13th. This area of low pressure was responsible for a significant snow and ice event across the Gulf Coast and Southeast states. Well ahead of the storm system, a heavy band of snowfall moved from south to north across the region. The snow began in the morning hours and continued through the mid afternoon hours. Snow fell at rates of up to three inches per hour, causing significant travel issues across the region.||Much of the region experienced a break in the steady snowfall between the mid-afternoon and early evening hours as the initial band of precipitation shifted north and east of the region and only periods of lighter snowfall occurred. However, the strengthening coastal storm allowed a second round of steady, heavy precipitation in the form of a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to begin in the late evening hours. This precipitation moved from south to north across the region for the overnight hours.  In addition, lightning and thunder accompanied the precipitation in some areas as well.||The precipitation tapered off from west to east during the morning hours on February 14th, as the storm moved northeast towards eastern New England and Atlantic Canada. By the time snow ended, 9 to 21 inches of snow was reported in Berkshire County. The highest amounts were across the high terrain of the Berkshires.  Very strong winds, gusting as high as 40 mph, occurred as the storm pulled away. This led to significant blowing and drifting of the snowfall through the entire day on February 14th.

A strong coastal storm brought wintry precipitation, heavy rain, and strong winds to much of southern New England.  A mix of precipitation brought freezing drizzle, snow, and sleet to northwestern portions of the area while heavy rain was widespread across southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, as well as portions of northern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility pole was downed onto wires on West Main Street in Hyannis.  In Sandwich, a tree was downed on Farmersville Road. An arctic air mass settled over the region during much of the week between Tuesday, January 21st and Friday, January 24th. Very cold air in place allowed for daytime high temperatures only in the single digits and teens and readings frequently below zero at night. With a persistent west to northwest breeze, wind chill values dropped to dangerously cold levels in the night time and morning hours. Wind chill values were -10 to -25 degrees over many areas, with wind chill readings as low as -30 degrees over the high terrain of the Berkshires. The most widespread lowest values occurred on the early morning of Wednesday, January 22nd. This long duration period of frigid arctic air resulted in shelters being opened across the region for the homeless. Temperatures finally moderated and wind became light by the end of the week, ending the dangerously low wind chill values.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front, bitter Arctic air moved into the region beginning in the early morning hours on Tuesday, January 7th. With strong westerly winds in place, this allowed for winds chill values to be as low as -10 to -25 degrees. Strong winds throughout the day on Tuesday, January 7th allowed the dangerously cold wind chill values to continue. Winds started to diminish on Tuesday night, but with continued very cold temperatures of below zero in many areas, the low wind chill values of less than -15 degrees continued. Although temperatures remaining frigid, high pressure over the region allowed for winds to be light on Wednesday, January 8th, ending the dangerously cold wind chill values.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting snowstorm occurring across all of western Massachusetts between the evening of New Years Day and the morning of January 3rd, 2014. ||A slow moving frontal boundary situated over the mid-Atlantic Region was in place on Wednesday, January 1st. An area of high pressure situated over southern Quebec allowed Arctic air to move down into the region. As a weak wave of low pressure developed along the front, moisture moved up and over the frontal boundary into the region. As a result, light snow broke out and gradually spread from southwest to northeast on the evening of Wednesday, January 1st and continued into the early morning hours of Thursday, January 2nd. During the daytime on January 2nd, there was a break in the steady snowfall, especially for the southern Berkshires, with occasional light snow showers and flurries and it remained quite cold, with temperatures only in the single digits and teens over much of the region. ||On the evening of Thursday, January 2nd, a new area of low pressure began to form of the mid-Atlantic coast. This brought some moisture from the Atlantic Ocean into the region, and a moderate snowfall developed over the entire area. The snow gradually tapered off to light snow and snow showers from west to east overnight as the low pressure area tracked east northeast away from the region. By the morning hours of Friday, January 3rd, snowfall amounts ranged from 4 inches in the southern Berkshires to 10 inches in the northern Berkshires. In addition, temperatures remained very cold and with a cold northwest wind, wind chill values were below zero.
A strong coastal storm moved up the east coast and over Nantucket bringing a heavy wet snow to much of the area. This resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow fell across western Essex County.  The heavy wet snow brought down a small tree in Andover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving coastal storm impacted all of western Massachusetts with a variety of winter weather for Tuesday, December 9th through early in the day on Thursday, December 11th. ||An area of low pressure developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast on Tuesday, December 9th. With cold air in place at the surface, the precipitation initially began as freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the early morning hours. Some sleet also mixed in, especially over the higher elevations. ||As surface temperatures slowly warmed above freezing, the precipitation changed to a plain cold rain by the late morning hours. This rain fell heavily at times for the afternoon and evening. Precipitation mixed with sleet and snow at times, especially for the higher elevations, but the bulk of the precipitation remained as a cold rain. ||On Tuesday night, precipitation continued mainly in the form of light rain for valley locations with a wintry mix over the high terrain. However, with colder air working into the area aloft, precipitation began to change to mainly snow for all areas for during the day on Wednesday, especially for the afternoon and evening hours. ||Precipitation began to taper off to just snow showers for Wednesday night and ended by Thursday morning. By the end of the storm, snowfall amounts were generally between 3 and 8 inches.  However, the highest elevations of the Berkshires in Savoy saw nearly 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level trough allowed an area of low pressure to develop along the New Jersey shore on the morning of Saturday, January 18th.  As this low pressure area rapidly developed, a fast moving area of snowfall moved northeast across western Massachusetts, beginning in the late morning hours.  Within this snowfall were localized areas of moderate to heavy snow.  Snow fell at rates in excess of one inch per hour in spots.  Snow tapered off by the late afternoon as the area of snow moved northeast into central and eastern New England, and amounts varied significantly across Berkshire County. Although most areas only saw a few inches, a small area in the higher elevations of southern Berkshire County received 6 to 10 inches of snowfall.  As a result of this snow, local newspaper media reported numerous traffic accidents and motorists stuck on snowy roads.

An arctic air mass over the region, combined with gusty west to northwest winds, allowed for very low wind chill values during the early morning hours on Tuesday, January 28th. Winds chill values were as low as -10 to -21 degrees, especially over the higher elevations. As temperatures warmed up and winds diminished, wind chill values rose to above dangerous levels by the late morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread snowfall occurred across all of western Massachusetts on Wednesday, February 5th. An area of low pressure moved across the Ohio Valley, bringing a period of moderate to heavy snowfall out well ahead of the storm during the early morning hours. Snow fell at rates in excess of one to two inches per hour, allowing for a messy commute during the morning hours. The snow changed to sleet and freezing rain across southern parts of the county, which continued through the morning hours at mainly light rates.||The low pressure area began to reform off the mid-Atlantic coast during the day and moved northeast towards the New England Coast by the evening hours. Some additional snow fell into the afternoon hours, before precipitation tapered off completely by the early evening hours.||By the time the storm finished, the entire region received a widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow. The heaviest amounts occurred across the high terrain of the Berkshires.
Low pressure moving off the mid-Atlantic coast intensified as it moved northeastward over Nantucket. This spread heavy snow across all of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to thirteen inches of snow fell across eastern Hampshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system passed south and east of southern New England bringing widespread rainfall to much of the region.  This system was anomalously moist with precipitable waters two to three standard deviations above normal for late March.  Anywhere from two to five inches of rain fell across southern New England with the highest amounts falling along the south coast of Rhode Island and Massachusetts.  This resulted in flash flooding across much of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued after three to five inches of rain fell across the region earlier in the day.  In New Bedford, Parker Street near New Bedford High School was closed due to continued flooding.  Dartmouth Street was washed out between Rockland and Spooner Streets.  The road was closed for repairs as the asphalt washed away.  In Acushnet, Main Street near Clems Way was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist tropical airmass in place over Southern New England aided in increasing rainfall rates of storms that developed ahead of a cold front.  Isolated flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Springfield, a car was stuck in flood waters on Bay Street. Also, the left high speed lane of Interstate 91 was blocked because of flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Southern New England, triggering slow moving showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these storms produced high rainfall rates, with more than 2 inches falling over a 1-2 hour period.  This resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Chicopee, portions of Granby Road, Nash Street, and Memorial Drive were flooded with nearly a foot of water.  A car was stuck in flood waters under the bridge at Prospect and North Chicopee Streets.  Boston Road in Wilbraham was flooded with a foot of water.  In Holyoke, a portion of Cabot Street was closed due to flooding.  A car was stuck in water at the underpass on Appleton Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Southern New England, triggering slow moving showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these storms produced high rainfall rates, with more than 2 inches falling over a 1-2 hour period.  This resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Northampton, Route 9 near the VA hospital was flooded with a foot of water.  Portions of Market and State Streets were flooded.  King Street was flooded in front of the McDonald's.  Multiple basements along Church Street were flooded with 6 to 8 feet of water.  Portions of route 202 in South Hadley were flooded with at least a foot of water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell in Leicester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a slow moving cold front, a southerly flow transported plenty of moisture into the region during the day on Tuesday, June 3rd. With a moist and unstable air mass in place, plenty of slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the approaching boundary. Some of these thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in a short period of time. This led to some flash flooding, especially in urban and poor drainage areas. In addition, a few of the thunderstorms also produced strong wind gusts, which caused damage to trees and power lines. By the late evening, the threat for thunderstorms ended, as the frontal boundary pushed through and less humid air moved into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lenox Road in Richmond was reportedly closed due to flash flooding from thunderstorm heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed in the eastern Great Lakes, providing enough lift and energy for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England.  With plenty of moisture, shear and instability in the region, some of these storms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds and flash flooding.  This began with a round of showers and thunderstorms in the morning, which mainly resulted in flash flooding.  This was followed with more shower and thunderstorm activity during the afternoon with damaging winds the main effect. ||One of the severe thunderstorms in the morning interacted with the marine front along the coast, allowing it to spin up into a rather brief tornado.  Despite the short path and duration of the tornado, the location in which it touched down, metro Boston, allowed it to do considerable damage before lifting.  Several injuries occurred, mainly from flying debris. This was the first tornado to hit Suffolk County since 1950, when modern record keeping began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Belmont, the common near Dark Street was nearly impassable due to flooding. In Winchester, a car was stuck in flood waters at the Swanson Street bridge and two cars were stuck at the Wedgemere Station bridge. Flash flooding made several roads impassable including a portion of Washington Street and Church Street at Lewis Street. In Medford, Windsor Road was flooded. In Somerville, Broomfield Road was flooded and impassable. The Lynnfield Tunnel on Route 1 in Lynnfield was flooded with three to four feet of water and was impassable. Flash flooding also affected Danvers where portions of Route 1 were closed and cars were stranded. In Peabody, Roosevelt Boulevard was flooded with four feet of water and Route 1 northbound by the Route 114 exit was closed due to three feet of water covering the roadway. Several cars were stranded in flood waters in Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front provided enough lift in a rather unstable environment to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Many of these storms produced strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were downed throughout Orange.

An approaching cold front provided enough lift in a rather unstable environment to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England. Many of these storms produced strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women were struck by lightning while walking towards the parking lot from the beach at Crane's Beach in Ipswich.  One was a 61 year old woman from Connecticut, the other a 69 year old woman from Massachusetts.  Both went into cardiac arrest and were given CPR before being transported to Beverly Hospital and then to Massachusetts General Hospital's burn unit. The 61 year old woman died of her injuries the evening of September 6th.  The 69 year old succumbed to her injuries a week later on September 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary, combined with plenty of heat and humidity, led to the development of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Some of the storms were severe and produced damaging winds across parts of central and northeast Massachusetts as well as localized flash flooding. Later at night, thunderstorms with torrential rainfall produced flash flooding near the South Coast and isolated damage from lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant urban flash flooding affected New Bedford from thunderstorms with torrential rainfall. Belleville Avenue was flooded at Hatch Street making the road impassable. Route 6 on the New Bedford side of the New Bedford Fairhaven Bridge was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed in the eastern Great Lakes, providing enough lift and energy for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England.  With plenty of moisture, shear and instability in the region, some of these storms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds and flash flooding.  This began with a round of showers and thunderstorms in the morning, which mainly resulted in flash flooding.  This was followed with more shower and thunderstorm activity during the afternoon with damaging winds the main effect. ||One of the severe thunderstorms in the morning interacted with the marine front along the coast, allowing it to spin up into a rather brief tornado.  Despite the short path and duration of the tornado, the location in which it touched down, metro Boston, allowed it to do considerable damage before lifting.  Several injuries occurred, mainly from flying debris. This was the first tornado to hit Suffolk County since 1950, when modern record keeping began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in Chelsea, where a window was blown out on Dudley Street. However, the overwhelming majority of damage occurred in Revere.  Most of the damage was rated EF-1, but EF-2 rated damage did occur near Revere Beach Parkway.  ||From Chelsea, the tornado moved across the Chelsea River, directly across the Paul Cronin Memorial Skating Rink.  Portions of the rink's roof were blown northeastward across Route 16.  Numerous houses on Revere Beach Parkway were damaged, with one roof blown completely off.  In that same area, large oak trees were snapped in half.  Numerous trees were downed, some of which were lifted up and then deposited on top of houses immediately behind Revere Beach Parkway.||The damage was widespread throughout much of the center of Revere.  The tornado traveled northward, following Route 107 or Broadway for the most part.  Windows were blown out of town hall and the roof was damaged.  A large birch tree was uprooted near town hall.  Sixty-four structures sustained damage that ranged from siding torn off to roofs lifted or blown off.  Thirteen of these structures (6 - 1 & 2 family homes, 3 - multifamily homes, and 4 commercial buildings) were deemed uninhabitable by building inspectors.  Several store signs were destroyed. Debris from homes collected on fences.  Trees fell on cars, a few of these crushed.||Revere High School also sustained damage, mainly to the exterior.  Side panels, fencing, sidewalks, and air handlers on the roof were damaged.||Police reported a car that had been overturned at the intersection of Revere Street and Carleton Avenue. Finally, near the rotary at Route 60 and Broadway, a billboard was blown onto several cars.||City officials estimated that city owned buildings alone sustained at least 1.5 to 2 million dollars of damage. ||While no major injuries were reported, several minor injuries occurred. An 86 year old woman suffered from a head laceration which she sustained from flying debris while standing outside a Dunkin Donuts.  A two week old baby girl strapped in the back seat of a truck was injured when a board flew through the rear windshield of the vehicle.  The board missed the infant but covered her in broken glass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was situated off the coast of the Northeastern United States on Sunday, November 2nd and was moving northeast towards southeastern Canada. The flow around this storm caused strong north to northwest winds up to 40 MPH across western Massachusetts. As a result of these winds, there were reports of trees and tree limbs down. Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England.  The strongest winds were along the east coast of Massachusetts where many trees were still fully leaved.  This resulted in significant tree damage along with power outages. Other streets in Lynn were flooded with three feet of water and the fire department responded to two calls of people stuck in their cars.  In Beverly, Federal Street between MacPherson Drive and Rantoul and Lothrop Street at Route 127 were closed due to flooding.  The fire department reported several streets flooded with at least 3 feet of water.  They responded to about a half dozen calls of people stuck in their cars.  In Salem, Highland Avenue was flooded out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Martha's Vineyard Airport (KMVY) recorded visibilities less than a quarter of a mile and winds gusting frequently over 35 mph and as high as 61 mph.  Sustained winds also were strong and the ASOS at MVY recorded a period of 48 mph sustained winds.  The strongest winds continued for another hour or two beyond the end of the blizzard conditions.  Snowfall amounts were modest though difficult to measure.  Two to four inches of snow was reported.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Systems (ASOS) at Chatham Municipal Airport (KCQX), Barnstable Municipal Airport in Hyannis (KHYA), and Cape Cod Coast Guard Air Station in Falmouth (KFMH) recorded visibilities at or below one quarter mile and frequent wind gusts over 35 mph.  These ranged from a peak wind gust of 49 mph at KCQX to 70 mph at KHYA.  Sustained winds were also very strong with the ASOS at KHYA recording 43 mph sustained winds for a period of time.  The strongest winds occurred coincident with the blizzard conditions, though gustier winds lasted a couple of hours longer than the snow did. Snowfall amounts were modest, though given the winds, very difficult to measure.  Roughly 4-6 of snow was reported.  Wires were downed onto a structure on Foster Road in Sandwich resulting in a fire.  Also, wires were downed in West Yarmouth.  A utility pole and wires were downed on Highbank Road in Yarmouth.  Wires were downed in Mashpee on Mashpee Neck Road and on Bank Street in Harwich.  A tree was downed onto a house on Casey Street in Yarmouth.  The top half of a tree was downed onto Bayport Road in West Harwich. A tree and wires were downed onto Depot Street in Dennis.  Trees were downed in Brewster on Main Street and on Long Pond Road near Route 137.  Wires were downed onto Stony Brook Road in Brewster.  A tree was downed onto a house in South Dennis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system passed south and east of southern New England bringing widespread rainfall to much of the region.  This system was anomalously moist with precipitable waters two to three standard deviations above normal for late March.  Anywhere from two to five inches of rain fell across southern New England with the highest amounts falling along the south coast of Rhode Island and Massachusetts.  This resulted in flash flooding across much of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell over a very brief period of time.  This resulted in flash flooding across much of Fall River and New Bedford.  In Fall River, several cars were stuck at the intersection of Plymouth Avenue and Globe Street.  Davol and Central Streets near the carousel were flooded and blocked off by local authorities.  Interstate 195 east at the base of the Braga Bridge was flooded out.  A car was stuck in flood waters at the intersection of Tucker Street and Rhode Island Avenue.  Langley Street was closed due to flooding.  In Somerset, one lane of Route 103 and the grass in the area was flooded with running water.  In New Bedford, Phillips Road was flooded near the 7-11 store because of clogged storm drains.  River Road was flooded from Howard Avenue to Sylvia Street.  Route 18 south was closed due to flooding near Purchase Street.  Four cars were stuck in flood waters on Acushnet Avenue.  Cars were going the wrong way on Route 18 in order to avoid the flooding. The intersection of Belleville Avenue and Sawyer Street was flooded with one and a half to two feet of water.  The intersections of Dean and Purchase Streets and Sawyer and Purchase Streets were flooded with three feet of water.  Multiple cars were stuck in flood waters throughout town.  The back of New Bedford Police Headquarters was flooded and impassable, police were advised to avoid the area and only park in front of the building.  The intersection of Coggeshall Street and Purchase Street was flooded and impassable.  The Route 18 ramp to Weld Street was closed due to flooding.  The intersection of Liberty and Hunter Streets was flooded with two and a half feet of water.  West Rodney French Boulevard was flooded and impassable.    In Acushnet, Hamlin Street was flooded near the bridge.  A portion of Middle Road was flooded and closed.  In Freetown, the ramp from Chace Road to Route 140 was closed due to flooding.  In Dartmouth, extensive flooding occurred on Hawley Drive and adjacent side streets.  Seminole Road at Masssasoit Lane was flooded and impassable.  Hawthorne Street west of Brownell Avenue and Clarence Street were flooded.  In Westport, Route 6 was closed near Boar's Head due to flooding.

This resulted in flash flooding across much of this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plymouth Avenue between Globe and Slade Streets was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind gusts up to 60 mph in Plymouth.  Additionally, the Automated Surface Observing System at Plymouth Municipal Airport recorded sustained wind speeds of 35 mph and wind gusts of 52 mph.  A tree and wires were downed onto State Road 3A in Plymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding occurred flooding portions of Warren Avenue in Plymouth.  Overwash and debris flooded onto Central Avenue and Cole Parkway in Scituate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Blue Hill Observatory and Science Center in Milton (KMQE) recorded sustained winds of 41 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding occurred at Straight Wharf, on Easy Street, and in the Brant Point section of Nantucket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Taunton Municipal Airport recorded wind gusts of 46 mph.  A tree was downed across Plain Street in Rehoboth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Ohio Valley towards the mid-Atlantic states on Wednesday, March 12th. Precipitation spread across the region from west to east during the early morning hours. The precipitation initially began as rain. As the morning hours went on and low pressure continue to strengthen, cold air at low levels began to move into the region from north to south. This allowed the rain to change to sleet and freezing rain during the afternoon hours.||The low pressure area moved towards the New York City area by early evening, and strong, northerly winds continued to transport colder air into the region. This allowed the wintry mix to change to a brief period of snow. The low pressure continued to move eastward towards the eastern New England coast for the overnight hours.||Steady precipitation tapered off during the overnight hours. By the time precipitation had ended, snowfall amounts ranged from a coating to two inches, with some light ice accretion occurring as well. In addition, gusty northwest winds occurred in the wake of the storm. These winds, along with unseasonably cold temperatures, allowed for a very cold day, despite it being mid March.
A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding occurred in Sandwich resulting in beach erosion to within seven feet of a house on White Cap Path and overwashing Town Neck Beach.  In Chatham, the Fishing Pier parking lot was flooded.  And in Dennis, Sesuit Harbor parking lot flooded and the Chapin Beach access ramp eroded away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Fayerweather Street in Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Logan International Airport recorded sustained winds of 38 mph and wind gusts of 54 mph.  These winds resulted in downed tree limbs and power lines on Allston and Rutherford Streets, as well as on Bronx Road.  In addition, these winds greatly exacerbated a structure fire in the Back Bay (ended up as a 9 alarm fire), in which 2 firefighters were killed and 13 others were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Systems at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded visibilities at or below one quarter mile and frequent wind gusts over 35 mph and as high as 82 mph at the peak of the blizzard conditions. Sustained winds were also very strong with the ASOS at KACK recording sustained wind speeds of 56 mph.  The strongest winds occurred coincident with the blizzard conditions, though gustier winds lasted a couple of hours longer than the snow did.  Snowfall amounts were highest on Nantucket with 8-10 inches reported.  A large branch was downed onto Highland Avenue, blocking the road.  Several large branches were downed on the east end of Nantucket Island.  Wires were downed, resulting in 1200 customers without power.  Milestone Road was blocked by multiple downed trees as well as snow drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States.  This storm brought extremely strong winds (gusting to over 80 mph) and heavy snow to the Cape and Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Fowler Road and onto a car on Mendon Street in Upton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Forest Street in Wilmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Southern New England, triggering slow moving showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these storms produced high rainfall rates, with more than 2 inches falling over a 1-2 hour period.  This resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Holyoke, underpasses near Sargeant Street were flooded and impassable.  Lyman and Canal Streets also were flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold air advection behind a mid level low resulted in strong winds at the surface.  This caused some wind damage mainly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Hanscom Field in Bedford, MA (KBED) recorded wind gusts of 51 mph.  It is estimated that winds gusted higher in other parts of Middlesex county where damage occurred.  A tree was downed onto a house and car on Governor Peabody Road in Billerica.  Old Sudbury Road in Lincoln was closed because of a downed tree and power lines.  A tree and wires were downed on Concord Street in Ashland.  In Burlington, a tree and wires were downed onto Wilmington Road.  A tree and power lines were downed onto Webster Road in Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface.  This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were downed onto wires and onto Forest Street in Bridgewater.  A tree was downed onto wires on Bristol Street in Lakeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface.  This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Plymouth Municipal Airport (KPYM) recorded wind gusts of 47 mph. A spotter in Plymouth estimated 50 mph wind gusts. Several trees were downed throughout Hingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface.  This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Taunton Municipal Airport (KTAN) recorded wind gusts to 43 mph.  Primary and secondary wires were downed onto Highland Street in Easton.  A tree was downed onto a house on Branch Street in Mansfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface.  This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Logan International Airport in Boston (KBOS) recorded wind gusts of 58 mph.  A power pole was downed on Central Avenue in Chelsea, the transformer caught fire.  Large branches brought down 5 pole lengths of wire on K and 7th Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface.  This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across the train tracks in Wilmington resulting in train and car traffic stopping.  Crossing gates were stuck in the down position.  Trees were downed on Rindge Avenue and Clay and Brewster Streets in Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface.  This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Blue Hill Observatory and Science Center (KMQE) in Milton recorded wind gusts of 66 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved up the eastern seaboard along a stalled frontal boundary during the morning on Wednesday, August 13th.  This system, aided by plenty of moisture off the Atlantic Ocean, produced heavy rain for several hours over the region.  Although extremely heavy rainfall fell just south of the area over Long Island, the rain was heavy enough in a short period of time to lead to flash flooding in southern Berkshire County during the late morning hours, with one road impassable due to high water.  Once the rain ended, water quickly receded across the area by around noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooper Hill Road at Rannapo Road in Ashley Falls was impassable due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in a warm, humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front.  Strong to damaging winds accompanied these thunderstorms resulting in tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A one foot diameter tree was downed onto a house on Day Street by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front, combined with tropical moisture in advance of Hurricane Arthur, produced two rounds of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The storms produced wind damage across much of the Bay State, primarily along and north of the Mass Pike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Brighton and Chelsea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed in the eastern Great Lakes, providing enough lift and energy for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England.  With plenty of moisture, shear and instability in the region, some of these storms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds and flash flooding.  This began with a round of showers and thunderstorms in the morning, which mainly resulted in flash flooding.  This was followed with more shower and thunderstorm activity during the afternoon with damaging winds the main effect. ||One of the severe thunderstorms in the morning interacted with the marine front along the coast, allowing it to spin up into a rather brief tornado.  Despite the short path and duration of the tornado, the location in which it touched down, metro Boston, allowed it to do considerable damage before lifting.  Several injuries occurred, mainly from flying debris. This was the first tornado to hit Suffolk County since 1950, when modern record keeping began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch and wires were downed on a car in front of the Brookfield Fire Station. Large branches were downed on Allen Road in Sturbridge.

In Gill, trees and wires were downed on River Street and West Gill Street. Thunderstorm winds also downed trees and wires on Chestnut Lane in Turners Falls, on Parmenter Road in Berndardston, and on Captain Bears Plain Road and Burnham Road in Northfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving out of the Great Lakes initiated showers and thunderstorms over New York and New England.  Enough instability was present in the region for some of these storms to become severe, producing strong to damaging winds and at least one funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines on South Meadow Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.  Trees and power lines along Route 20 were downed as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on High Street was downed by thunderstorm winds, blocking the road.  Trees and wires along Prospect Avenue were downed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place during the early morning hours on Wednesday, June 18th. A strong cold front moved across the region from west to east shortly after midnight during the early morning hours. Along and just ahead of this boundary, a line of severe thunderstorms in the form of a squall line raced from west to east across the area. These storms produced significant wind damage across eastern New York. The thunderstorms weakened somewhat as they reached the border with western New England as instability started to wane, but still caused damage to trees in a few locations. The threat for thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front by the late night hours, as drier and less humid air worked into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree and wires were reported down in Egremont Plain due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving out of the Great Lakes initiated showers and thunderstorms over New York and New England.  Enough instability was present in the region for some of these storms to become severe, producing strong to damaging winds and at least one funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a trailer home on Springfield Street.  A tree on Meadow Road was downed by thunderstorm winds.

Grafton Street was flooded with 8 inches of water from Barclay Street to Interstate 290 with numerous manhole covers popped off.  Harding Street and West Boylston Street were flooded with up to 8 inches of water.  Cars were stuck in flood waters on Quinsigamond Avenue and Plantation Street.  Numerous basements were flooded on Water Street.  Several lanes of Interstate 290 were flooded at Lincoln Square.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees on Silver Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.  Trees were downed onto wires on Main Street and North Westfield Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High shear and decent moisture and instability in advance of a cold front allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on several streets, including Park and Main.  A tree was downed onto two cars on Amostown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed in the eastern Great Lakes, providing enough lift and energy for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England.  With plenty of moisture, shear and instability in the region, some of these storms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds and flash flooding.  This began with a round of showers and thunderstorms in the morning, which mainly resulted in flash flooding.  This was followed with more shower and thunderstorm activity during the afternoon with damaging winds the main effect. ||One of the severe thunderstorms in the morning interacted with the marine front along the coast, allowing it to spin up into a rather brief tornado.  Despite the short path and duration of the tornado, the location in which it touched down, metro Boston, allowed it to do considerable damage before lifting.  Several injuries occurred, mainly from flying debris. This was the first tornado to hit Suffolk County since 1950, when modern record keeping began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Todd Road, setting it on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms across interior southern New England, some of which produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike was determined to be the cause of a fire that destroyed a 6,500 square foot home on North Farms Road in Northampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, coastal flooding, and strong winds to Massachusetts.  Snow amounts varied widely, from roughly six inches across the east slopes of the Berkshires to nearly two feet in coastal Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding impacted eastern Norfolk County. In Cohasset, Atlantic Avenue was closed as was Margin Street and Jerusalem Road at Bow Street. In Quincy, the storm surge backed into the Neponset River, which produced considerable flooding on Hancock Street. One car was stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, coastal flooding, and strong winds to Massachusetts.  Snow amounts varied widely, from roughly six inches across the east slopes of the Berkshires to nearly two feet in coastal Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding occurred on Washington Street between Blue Fish Reservoir and Gurnet Road in Duxbury.  King Caesar Road was also flooded and was closed.  In Scituate, Cole Parkway was closed due to coastal flooding. Oceanside and Lighthouse Roads had minor splashover but were passable.  Coastal flooding occurred on Rockland Street and Kilby Street in Hingham with backyards flooding up to the foundations of houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved off the DelMarVa peninsula and moved northeastward passing southeast of southern New England. This brought strong winds and heavy snow to the southern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to ten inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County. A tree, branches, and a few wires were downed due to the weight of the snow and winds gusting to around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved off the DelMarVa peninsula and moved northeastward passing southeast of southern New England. This brought strong winds and heavy snow to the southern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to ten inches of snow fell across southern Bristol County.  A few branches and wires were downed due to the heavy snow and wind gusts to around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved off the DelMarVa peninsula and moved northeastward passing southeast of southern New England. This brought strong winds and heavy snow to the southern portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell across western Plymouth County.  A single tree and a few wires were downed due to the weight of the snow and winds gusting to around 40 mph in Halifax and Plympton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought six to twelve inches of snow across much of southern New England.  Lesser amounts fell east of the Interstate 95 corridor where snow changed to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded sustained wind speeds of 41 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, coastal flooding, and strong winds to Massachusetts.  Snow amounts varied widely, from roughly six inches across the east slopes of the Berkshires to nearly two feet in coastal Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell across Nantucket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, coastal flooding, and strong winds to Massachusetts.  Snow amounts varied widely, from roughly six inches across the east slopes of the Berkshires to nearly two feet in coastal Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to seventeen inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, coastal flooding, and strong winds to Massachusetts.  Snow amounts varied widely, from roughly six inches across the east slopes of the Berkshires to nearly two feet in coastal Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to sixteen inches of snow fell across Cape Cod.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, coastal flooding, and strong winds to Massachusetts.  Snow amounts varied widely, from roughly six inches across the east slopes of the Berkshires to nearly two feet in coastal Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observing System in Marshfield (KGHG) recorded frequent wind gusts up to 43 mph.  This combined with heavy snow and resulted in near zero visibilities.  The combination of winds and heavy snow downed wires on Ocean Street in Marshfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing south of Long Island and moving northeastward across southern New England brought six to twelve inches of snow to northern Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire with lesser amounts elsewhere in the region.  Much of this fell during the evening rush hour impacting travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to eleven inches of snow fell across northern Worcester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two communications tower located on top of Florida Mountain in North Adams were damaged due to gusty east to southeasterly winds during the overnight hours between Saturday, March 29th and Sunday, March 30th.  The combinations of strong winds and heavy rainfall in excess of one inch led to the collapse of two radio towers.  The loss of these two towers led to an interruption of cell and internet service over the region until a temporary tower was set up by the evening of Sunday, March 30th.  Winds peaked at North Adams airport at 51 mph, with other nearby stations overnight reporting gusts between 26 mph and 40 mph.

A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th. As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary. As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas. Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well. In addition, a few of the storms produced damaging lightning strikes as well. ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brook was overflowing Shore Road in Pittsfield due to thunderstorm heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed in a warm, humid airmass ahead of an approaching cold front.  Strong to damaging winds accompanied these thunderstorms resulting in tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful microburst occurred in Bedford with estimated 90 to 100 mph wind gusts.  Approximately 50 to 70 trees were blown down, most of them healthy pine trees 100 feet tall.  The trees were blown down, snapped at least halfway up, or uprooted.  Eight trees fell onto houses causing significant damage.  On Hilltop Drive and Hemlock Lane alone, more than two dozen pine trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving up the east coast brought a soaking rain and strong winds to much of southern New England.  The strongest winds were along the east coast of Massachusetts where many trees were still fully leaved.  This resulted in significant tree damage along with power outages.  In addition, both downed leaves from the storm and naturally fallen leaves from before the storm clogged storm drains which resulted in street flooding, particularly across northeastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 37 was flooded in Holbrook.  Route 28 was closed in both directions between Royal Crest Drive and Gordon Street in Randolph due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with very cold air aloft to produce showers and thunderstorms across much of Southern New England. Many of these storms produced large hail.
High shear and decent moisture and instability in advance of a cold front allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed onto a house on Ambrose Street. Wires were downed on Prospect Avenue and Hunter's Place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving out of the Great Lakes initiated showers and thunderstorms over New York and New England.  Enough instability was present in the region for some of these storms to become severe, producing strong to damaging winds and at least one funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and power lines on Elmwood Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front, combined with tropical moisture in advance of Hurricane Arthur, produced two rounds of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The storms produced wind damage across much of the Bay State, primarily along and north of the Mass Pike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Dracut. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across New York and New England triggered a few showers and thunderstorms across western Massachusetts.  Some tree damage resulted and one house was struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Cornell Street, resulting in minor shingle damage and fire damage to the exterior of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm brought wintry precipitation, heavy rain, and strong winds to much of southern New England.  A mix of precipitation brought freezing drizzle, snow, and sleet to northwestern portions of the area while heavy rain was widespread across southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, as well as portions of northern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retaining wall the length of two rail cars collapsed just west of the Waverly MBTA station in Belmont, blocking the train tracks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning and again in the afternoon as a slow moving frontal boundary crossed southern New England. A tropical airmass was in place which led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Taunton, flash flooding stranded cars on Forest Street where the flood waters rose up to car doors. County Street was closed due to flooding at Ingell Street and in front of Pete's Market. In East Taunton, Middleboro Avenue was flooded near the Berkeley line and was described as a big lake. In Raynham, portions of North Street and Orchard Street were flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over western Massachusetts during the afternoon and evening hours on Monday, July 7th, which allowed the atmosphere to become quite unstable. As an upper level shortwave trough moved towards the area, clusters of thunderstorms developed over eastern New York and moved into western Massachusetts by the evening hours. These thunderstorms, although not overly tall, were able to bring very strong winds aloft down to the surface, producing damaging wind gusts to trees and power lines. The threat for severe thunderstorms ended by the late evening hours, as the shortwave trough moved east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Dalton due to thunderstorm winds.

These thunderstorms, although not overly tall, were able to bring very strong winds aloft down to the surface, producing damaging wind gusts to trees and power lines. The threat for severe thunderstorms ended by the late evening hours, as the shortwave trough moved east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Pittsfield due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was situated over the Great Lakes region on Sunday, July 27th. An upper level shortwave, combined with an approaching warm front, allowed for a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms to break out during the afternoon hours on July 27th over eastern New York, which spread into western Massachusetts during the late afternoon hours. In addition to straight-line wind damage and large hail, the area of thunderstorms also produced an EF1 tornado in Dalton, causing damage to trees and homes. Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms led to flash flooding, especially in the urbanized areas around Pittsfield.||As the strong area of low pressure moved across upstate New York on Monday, July 28th, some additional thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon hours. Trees were downed due to thunderstorms winds across parts of Berkshire County, as the storm's cold front moved through the area. With additional heavy rainfall occurring, flash flooding was renewed in some parts of the Berkshires once again, as two day rainfall totals were in excess of three inches in the Pittsfield area. Cooler and less humid air worked into the area behind the front for Monday night, ending the threat for severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Pittsfield were closed due to flash flooding from thunderstorm heavy rainfall, including Hubbard Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms heavily damaged a 1.5 mile stretch of the Berry Pond Circuit Road in the Pittsfield State Forest in Pittsfield.  As a result of the flash flooding, 13 popular campsites at Berry Pond had to be closed for the remainder of the summer season due to the damaged access road.  A local newspaper reported that when the flash flooding occurred, campers had to be evacuated before water cascading down the mountain gouged out the paved road.   The damage to the road and eleven stone culverts was estimated to be significant and will cost between $173,000 and $277,000 to repair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd. As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over upstate New York and moved eastward during the late afternoon hours. Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over western Massachusetts during the early evening hours. A few of the storms produced gusty winds and large hail, especially across southern Berkshire County.  In addition, the storms produced heavy rainfall in a short period of time.  This lead to some flooding across the urbanized parts of the Berkshires as well. ||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Mill River.

In addition, a few of the storms produced damaging lightning strikes as well. ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in Savoy.  A portion of Route 116 near the Savoy-Chester town line was damaged due to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from repeated thunderstorms led to flash flooding in Sheffield.  South Undermountain Road was washed out as a result of the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike downed a tree on Route 43 (Water Street) in Williamstown.  The road was briefly closed as a result of the downed tree, which had to be removed.  The local utility company reported 239 customers without power in the town.

The landscaped gardens had been destroyed and the dirt road leading to the mansion was washed out and impassable due to the force of the flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was situated over the Great Lakes region on Sunday, July 27th. An upper level shortwave, combined with an approaching warm front, allowed for a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms to break out during the afternoon hours on July 27th over eastern New York, which spread into western Massachusetts during the late afternoon hours. In addition to straight-line wind damage and large hail, the area of thunderstorms also produced an EF1 tornado in Dalton, causing damage to trees and homes. Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms led to flash flooding, especially in the urbanized areas around Pittsfield.||As the strong area of low pressure moved across upstate New York on Monday, July 28th, some additional thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon hours. Trees were downed due to thunderstorms winds across parts of Berkshire County, as the storm's cold front moved through the area. With additional heavy rainfall occurring, flash flooding was renewed in some parts of the Berkshires once again, as two day rainfall totals were in excess of three inches in the Pittsfield area. Cooler and less humid air worked into the area behind the front for Monday night, ending the threat for severe weather and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and on power lines on Mountain Road in Lanesborough due to thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined that an EF1 tornado occurred in the Greenridge section of Dalton.  The tornado began in Greenridge park on South Street and moved southeast up a hill for one-quarter of a mile, before lifting behind residences on Lindsay Drive.  The tornado downed a path of trees through a forest, clearing an area that was 10 to 20 yards wide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined that a microburst occurred in the Greenridge section of Dalton.  Most of the direct damage from the microburst was to several hardwood and softwood trees, although one roof was partially lifted up on a house as a result of the wind as well.  Newspaper media reported that at least twenty trees were downed in the area and power lines were downed as well.  Minor damage to homes, mainly due to falling trees, occurred at least six residences.  An EF1 tornado also occurred nearby, but the majority of the damage was attributed to the microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Tuesday, July 8th. The region was ahead of a strong cold front that was moving eastward from the Great Lakes Region. Aided by a strong jet streak aloft and a potent upper level shortwave trough, the cold front triggered a line of thunderstorms that produced damaging winds across upstate New York during the afternoon and early evening hours.||As the line of thunderstorms reached western New England by the late evening, they weakened considerably. However, one portion of the line remained strong enough to produce damaging winds in Williamstown.  As the thunderstorms continued to move eastward towards central Massachusetts, they all dropped below severe limits, ending the threat for dangerous weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed due to thunderstorm winds on a house on Cole Avenue in Williamstown.
